ocr.text: The data presented in Table 1 offers a comprehensive overview of the sex distribution within the study's intervention and
control groups, highlighting critical insights into participant demographics. Specifically, the variable of interest—sex—has been
meticulously categorized into female and male participants, providing a clear representation of the gender composition in both
groups. In the intervention cohort, which consists of 47 participants, 27 are female, accounting for 57% of the group.
Variable  Intervention (n = 47)  Control (n = 54)
Sex (female/male (percent female))  27/20 (57 %)  31/23 (57 %)
Steps per day, pedometry  5390 (2791)  4980 (2763)
Physical activity diary
Sessions/week of at least moderate intensitya  2 (1–5)  2 (1–5)
Minutes/week of at least moderate intensitya  120 (0–220)  130 (40–215)
Questionnaire
Sitting time (hours/day)  5 (3–7)  5 (4–7)
Leisure time physical activity
Sedentary leisure time  13 %  11 %
Light activities ≥2 h/week  64 %  59 %
Regular moderate PA 1 to 2 · ≥30 min/week  15 %  20 %
Regular exercise ≥3 · ≥30 min/week  4 %  9 %
Follow-up data unavailable (n)  2  0
Similarly, the control group, comprising 54 participants, also reflects a balanced gender distribution, with 31 females,
representing 57% of the total. This consistent percentage of female participants across both groups underscores the study's
commitment to maintaining gender parity, which is essential for ensuring the generalizability of the findings. Furthermore, this
demographic balance allows for a more nuanced analysis of the intervention’s effects, as it mitigates potential biases that may
arise from uneven sex ratios. The data thus serves as a foundational element for subsequent analyses, establishing a robust
framework for evaluating the impact of the intervention on both male and female participants, as well as contributing to a more
comprehensive understanding of the study's outcomes.
